HDFCBANK.name: HDFC Bank
HDFCBANK.source_note: HDFC Bank Q3 FY26 results filing (SEC Form 6-K) and Q4 FY26 investor presentation
HDFCBANK.business_core: HDFC Bank takes public deposits and lends the money back out across home loans, vehicle loans, credit cards, SME and corporate credit. It earns mainly on the spread between what it pays depositors and what it charges borrowers (net interest margin), plus fees from cards, insurance distribution and transaction banking. It absorbed parent HDFC Ltd, India's largest mortgage lender, in a July 2023 merger.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: On interest-earning assets. Core lending profit margin.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Share of loans categorised as bad, before provisions.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Bad loans after the bank's own provisioning cushion.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Share of deposits that are 'cheap' current + savings money.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Safety cushion vs. an 11.9% regulatory minimum.

bull: Net NPA of just 0.42% — asset quality is exceptionally clean by Indian banking standards.
Capital cushion (19.9% CAR) sits well above the 11.9% regulatory floor, room to grow without raising fresh capital.
Advances (+11.9% YoY) and deposits (+11.6% YoY) are growing in lockstep — balanced, not over-extended growth.
bear: CASA ratio of 33.6% is moderate, not best-in-class — more reliance on costlier term deposits than some peers.
The HDFC Ltd merger (July 2023) means pre-FY24 multi-year comparisons aren't meaningful — read older trend data with care.
Sector-wide NIM compression as deposit costs catch up with loan repricing is worth watching over the next few quarters.

value_chain.position: No physical value chain — banks face a platform disintermediation dynamic instead. HDFC Bank holds the customer's account and the underlying money, but the customer-facing 'app layer' for everyday payments increasingly belongs to someone else.
value_chain.note: BIS research on UPI confirms this directly: banks hold a small share of the UPI transaction-initiation market by value, even as the dominant non-bank apps (Google Pay, PhonePe) capture the customer relationship at the point of use.

tech_geo_tags.label: Platform disintermediation: UPI's customer-facing layer is increasingly owned by non-bank apps, not banks themselves, per BIS research
Account Aggregator framework: facilitated an estimated $19.5bn in loans in FY24-25 via consent-based data sharing — a new lending distribution channel banks can tap, not purely a threat
Zero-MDR policy: UPI merchant fees remain government-mandated at zero — banks carry real infrastructure cost on this rail with no direct transaction revenue from it

HDFCBANK.moat_note: Scale + branch network (9,455+ branches) + brand trust. India's largest private bank by assets and one of three systemically important banks.

ICICIBANK.name: ICICI Bank
ICICIBANK.source_note: ICICI Bank's own Q3 FY26 performance review (icicibank.com) and Screener.in's Q3 FY26 ratio summary
ICICIBANK.business_core: ICICI Bank runs the same core model as any large bank — deposits fund a loan book spanning retail mortgages, vehicle loans, SME and corporate credit — and profits from the interest spread plus fee income from cards, wealth management and insurance cross-sold through its digital apps (iMobile, InstaBIZ).

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Highest among India's large banks this quarter.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Share of loans categorised as bad, before provisions.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Bad loans after the bank's own provisioning cushion.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Average CASA ratio for the quarter.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Safety cushion vs. an 11.70% regulatory minimum.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in domestic loan portfolio.

bull: NIM of 4.30% is the highest among India's large banks — directly boosts core lending profitability.
Net NPA of just 0.37%, with a 75.4% provisioning coverage ratio — an unusually strong cushion against future shocks.
Loan growth (11.5% YoY) is broad-based across retail, SME and business banking, not reliant on one segment.
bear: A one-time RBI-mandated provision (₹1,283 crore on agri-sector loans) dented Q3 profit growth — regulatory directives can bite even strong banks.
CASA ratio of 39% has been gradually moderating as depositors chase higher-yielding term deposits, a sector-wide funding-cost headwind.
Contingent liabilities are very large in absolute terms — typical for a bank this size, but worth understanding before treating it as a red flag on its own.

value_chain.position: No physical value chain — like all banks it faces platform disintermediation: it holds the customer account and the money, while the everyday-payments app layer increasingly belongs to third-party UPI apps. ICICI has answered with one of the strongest in-house digital franchises (iMobile, InstaBIZ) among private banks.
value_chain.note: A well-diversified retail and corporate book — no single-segment concentration flagged.

tech_geo_tags.label: Platform disintermediation: non-bank UPI apps (PhonePe, Google Pay) own the customer-facing payments layer, and zero-MDR means banks carry the UPI rails cost with no direct fee revenue
Account Aggregator plus a strong in-house digital stack let ICICI use consent-based data for underwriting rather than only defend against fintechs
RBI policy transmission: the June 2026 repo hold at 5.50% with a raised inflation forecast means deposit-cost relief is slower than hoped — a sector-wide margin factor

ICICIBANK.moat_note: Brand trust + scale + a deep retail/digital franchise. India's second-largest private bank, consistently gaining share in retail and MSME lending.

SBIN.name: State Bank of India
SBIN.source_note: SBI's own Q3 FY26 press release and analyst presentation (sbi.bank.in)
SBIN.business_core: State Bank of India is the country's largest bank by assets and branch reach, majority government-owned. It takes deposits and lends across retail, agriculture, SME and corporate India, and earns additional fee income through subsidiaries like SBI Life, SBI Cards and SBI Mutual Fund that sit alongside the core lending business.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Whole-bank NIM; management guides to keep it above 3%.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Lowest level in over two decades for SBI.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Lowest level in over two decades for SBI.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Held nearly flat even as the sector lost CASA share to term deposits.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Thinnest cushion of the three banks here, still above the regulatory floor.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in gross advances, ahead of prior guidance.

bull: Net NPA (0.39%) and Gross NPA (1.57%) are both at their lowest in over two decades — a structural improvement, not a one-off quarter.

CASA ratio held nearly flat at 39.13% even as the sector saw outflows to term deposits — its 23,000+ branch network and government/PSU banking relationships are a funding edge competitors can't easily copy.
Loan book grew 15.14% YoY, ahead of its own guidance, prompting management to raise FY26 credit-growth guidance to 13–15%.
bear: Capital adequacy (14.04%) is comfortably above the regulatory minimum but is the thinnest cushion of the three banks on this dashboard — less room to absorb a shock without raising capital.
NIM has been on a gradual multi-quarter downtrend as deposit costs catch up; management is guiding to hold it 'above 3%,' not expand it.
As a majority government-owned bank, financial-inclusion mandates and policy priorities can occasionally take precedence over pure profit-maximisation — a structural, not one-off, consideration.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic — but as a majority government-owned bank, SBI also carries the state's policy priorities (financial-inclusion mandates, social schemes, sovereign borrowing programmes) alongside commercial goals. Its BHIM and YONO stack keep it closer to the payments layer than most.

tech_geo_tags.label: Government majority ownership: SBI executes national financial-inclusion and social-scheme mandates a purely commercial bank would not — a dual objective that cuts both ways
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR: SBI carries enormous UPI rails volume with no direct transaction revenue
Sovereign backing plus unmatched branch and agent reach make it the default channel for government and PSU flows

SBIN.moat_note: Sovereign-backed trust + an unmatched branch and agent network (23,000+ branches, more than the next five banks combined) + default banker for government and PSU flows.

KOTAKBANK.name: Kotak Mahindra Bank
KOTAKBANK.source_note: Kotak Mahindra Bank's own Q3 FY26 press release (kotak.bank.in) and corroborating market coverage
KOTAKBANK.business_core: Kotak Mahindra Bank grew out of a diversified NBFC before converting to a bank — its book still leans toward secured retail and vehicle lending alongside corporate credit. Beyond deposits and loans, the wider Kotak group earns from broking, asset management and insurance, cross-sold to the same customer base.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Down from 4.93% a year ago — margin compression is the key watch item.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Highest of the banks tracked here.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Strongest capital cushion of any bank on this dashboard.

bull: Capital adequacy of 22.6% is the strongest cushion of any bank on this dashboard — more than double the regulatory minimum.
Net NPA (0.31%) and Gross NPA (1.30%) both improved YoY — a genuinely strengthening asset-quality trend, not just a stable one.
CASA ratio of 41.3% is the highest of the five banks tracked here — a structural low-cost-funding edge.
bear: NIM compressed sharply to 4.54% from 4.93% a year ago, and management itself flagged only a 'moderate' recovery ahead, not a sharp one.
Provisions jumped on stress in credit cards, microfinance, and retail commercial vehicles — a segment to keep watching, not a closed issue.
The stock has historically traded at a premium valuation versus peers, leaving less room for error if growth or margins disappoint further.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the same platform-disintermediation dynamic as every bank. Kotak's distinctive sensitivity: in 2024 the RBI temporarily barred it from onboarding customers digitally and issuing new credit cards over IT-resilience gaps — a reminder that for a digital-first bank, tech-compliance is itself a core operational dependency.

tech_geo_tags.label: Regulatory-tech dependency: the 2024 RBI restriction on digital onboarding and new cards showed how tech-resilience compliance can directly gate growth for a digital-first bank
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR — the same UPI dynamic facing all banks, with customer-facing payments owned by non-bank apps
A premium, high-CASA deposit franchise plus Account Aggregator access give it a low-cost base and consent-based data for underwriting

KOTAKBANK.moat_note: Digital-first franchise + disciplined underwriting culture + one of the strongest capital positions in Indian banking — historically commands a premium valuation for exactly that reason.

AXISBANK.name: Axis Bank
AXISBANK.source_note: Axis Bank's own Q3 FY26 results announcement (axis.bank.in) and corroborating market coverage
AXISBANK.business_core: Axis Bank is India's third-largest private bank: it takes deposits and lends across retail, SME and corporate banking, earning the interest spread plus fee income from cards, transaction banking and third-party product distribution (insurance, mutual funds) sold to its customers.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Down from 3.73% the prior quarter — margin pressure persists.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Bank calls this 'among the best across large peer banks,' though it's eased from 41% a year ago.

bull: Gross NPA (1.40%) and Net NPA (0.42%) both improved sequentially and YoY — asset quality genuinely getting better.
Net profit rebounded sharply (+27.5% QoQ) on a 36.7% drop in provisions — the earlier credit-cost spike looks like it's normalising.
CASA ratio of 39% remains, in the bank's own words, 'among the best across large peer banks' despite the sector-wide funding-cost squeeze.
bear: NIM compressed to 3.64% from 3.73% the prior quarter, with no clear sign yet of a quick reversal.
Provision coverage ratio fell sharply to 70% from 78% a year ago, even as headline NPA ratios improved — a thinner safety cushion against any fresh stress.
CASA has slid for several straight quarters (41% a year ago to 39% now) — sector-wide, but worth tracking closely for Axis specifically.

value_chain.position: No physical chain, but Axis sits unusually close to the payments layer itself — it is one of the largest UPI payer-PSP (sponsor) banks by volume, routing a big share of UPI payer-side transactions. That is strategically central to the rails, yet revenue-light under zero-MDR.

tech_geo_tags.label: Deep UPI PSP role: Axis processes a large share of UPI payer-side volume as a sponsor bank — central to the rails, but carrying real infrastructure cost under zero-MDR
Platform disintermediation: customer-facing payments increasingly owned by non-bank apps even though banks hold the underlying accounts
Account Aggregator plus a heavy fintech-partnership model give Axis consent-based data and extended distribution reach

AXISBANK.moat_note: Scale as India's third-largest private bank + a strong digital/UPI franchise (~36-39% market share in UPI Payer PSP volume) + a diversified retail/SME/corporate mix that spreads concentration risk.

BAJFINANCE.name: Bajaj Finance
BAJFINANCE.source_note: Bajaj Finance's own Q3 FY26 investor presentation (bajajfinserv.in) and corroborating market coverage
BAJFINANCE.business_core: Bajaj Finance is a non-deposit-taking NBFC — instead of collecting public deposits like a bank, it borrows wholesale from banks and bond markets and re-lends that money at a spread. Its signature business is point-of-sale consumer lending (EMI financing for electronics and appliances in-store), alongside personal loans, SME loans and mortgages.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Not separately headlined this quarter — management said NIM 'remained steady,' without a number. Shown as — rather than guessed.

cost_of_borrowing_pct.label: Cost of Borrowing
cost_of_borrowing_pct.note: What it costs Bajaj Finance to fund itself — the NBFC equivalent of a bank's CASA ratio. Lower is better.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: CRAR including Tier-II capital; Tier-1 alone was 20.60%.

loan_book_growth.label: AUM Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in Assets Under Management — an NBFC's equivalent of loan book growth.

bull: Asset quality keeps improving, not just holding steady: Gross NPA 1.21% and Net NPA 0.47%, both down from the prior quarter.
AUM grew 22% YoY, on track for management's own ~22-23% guidance and crossing ₹5 lakh crore in scale shortly after this quarter.
Capital adequacy of 21.45% is far above the regulatory minimum, with cost of borrowing easing slightly (7.45%, down 7bps QoQ) even as rates stayed elevated sector-wide.
bear: Reported consolidated profit actually fell ~6% YoY this quarter due to an accelerated provisioning charge — core profit (excluding that one-off) still grew 23%, but headline optics were weak.

Unlike every bank on this dashboard, Bajaj Finance doesn't headline a single NIM% figure — a reminder that NBFC disclosures aren't a drop-in replacement for bank disclosures, even when the underlying business is healthy.
MSME growth has been deliberately muted for several quarters (management itself guides 2-3 more soft quarters there) — a self-imposed brake, not a crisis, but a real near-term growth drag.

value_chain.position: No physical chain — an NBFC's value chain is funding cost versus lending yield. Bajaj borrows wholesale (it has no CASA deposits) and lends across consumer and SME, so it is structurally more rate- and funding-sensitive than a bank, and squarely inside the unsecured-consumer-credit segment RBI has been tightening.

tech_geo_tags.label: Unsecured-consumer-credit regulation: RBI raised risk weights on unsecured consumer loans (Nov 2023) over overheating, and FY26 is flagged as a stress year for exactly this segment — Bajaj's large consumer book sits in the crosshairs
Funding-cost sensitivity: with no low-cost CASA base, Bajaj's margins move with wholesale funding costs and the RBI rate path (repo held at 5.50%, June 2026)
Scale plus an early, aggressive bet on AI in underwriting (its FinAI platform) give it a data and cost moat smaller NBFCs cannot match

BAJFINANCE.moat_note: Scale + a 115 million+ customer franchise + an early, aggressive bet on embedding AI into underwriting and collections (its own 'FinAI' platform) — a distribution and data moat that's hard for smaller NBFCs to replicate.

CHOLAFIN.name: Cholamandalam Investment & Finance
CHOLAFIN.source_note: Cholamandalam's own Q3 FY26 press release (murugappa.com) and corroborating market/analyst coverage
CHOLAFIN.business_core: Cholamandalam Investment & Finance (Murugappa Group) is an NBFC built mainly around vehicle finance — commercial vehicles, cars and tractors — plus home loans and SME lending. Like Bajaj Finance, it borrows wholesale and lends at a spread, so its profit is driven by loan-book growth, asset quality through vehicle-financing cycles, and its own cost of borrowing.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Not headlined as an absolute number this quarter — management discussed only the direction ('NIM expansion expected'), not a figure. Shown as — rather than guessed.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: RBI/IRACP-norms basis (the more conservative of two bases Chola reports — its Ind-AS 'Stage 3' figure runs lower, around 3.4%).

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: RBI/IRACP-norms basis, up slightly from 3.07% the prior quarter.

cost_of_borrowing_pct.label: Cost of Borrowing
cost_of_borrowing_pct.note: Not disclosed as a single absolute figure this quarter in available sources — shown as — rather than estimated.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Closest available figure is as of late Nov 2025, just before quarter-end — well above the 15% regulatory minimum.

loan_book_growth.label: AUM Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in Assets Under Management, consistent across the surrounding quarters.

bull: AUM grew ~21% YoY with broad-based strength — SME loans up 41%, home loans up 23%, and its largest segment (vehicle finance) regaining momentum after a soft patch.
Capital adequacy of ~19.8% is comfortably above the 15% regulatory minimum, real headroom to sustain 20%+ AUM growth ambitions.
Profitability stayed strong (ROE around 18-19%) even while investing in newer, faster-growing lines like gold loans and SME finance.
bear: Asset quality is the recurring worry: Gross NPA under RBI norms (4.63%) reads meaningfully worse than the Ind-AS 'Stage 3' figure (~3.4%) the company also reports — know which number you're looking at before comparing Chola's asset quality to a bank's GNPA%, since they're not computed the same way.
Vehicle finance, still its single largest segment, has seen rising delinquencies tied to weaker rural economies more than once in the past year — a cyclical risk, not a one-off.
Trades at a premium valuation (P/E well above peer median) — several analysts have flagged this as pricing in close-to-flawless execution, leaving little room for a misstep.

value_chain.position: No physical chain — funding cost versus lending yield. Chola is anchored in vehicle finance, so it rides the commercial-vehicle and auto cycle, with a large rural and semi-urban customer base sensitive to the monsoon and rural income.

tech_geo_tags.label: Vehicle and CV-cycle dependence: its largest segment tracks freight demand and the auto cycle — a slowdown flows through faster than for a diversified lender
Rural income and climate sensitivity: a big rural and semi-urban book is exposed to monsoon-linked income volatility, a structural risk RBI has flagged for FY26
Diversification into home loans, SME and gold loans is reducing single-cycle dependence, and funding access stays strong under the Murugappa Group umbrella

CHOLAFIN.moat_note: A decades-old vehicle-finance distribution network (1,600+ branches) under the Murugappa Group umbrella, now diversifying into home loans, SME and gold loans to reduce reliance on any single credit cycle — though vehicle finance concentration is still its biggest single swing factor.

SHRIRAMFIN.name: Shriram Finance
SHRIRAMFIN.source_note: Shriram Finance's own Q3 FY26 earnings call and CARE/ICRA rating notes (Dec 2025)
SHRIRAMFIN.business_core: Shriram Finance is India's largest NBFC focused on financing used commercial vehicles — trucks bought by small, individual transport operators who banks typically won't lend to. It's a relationship- and cash-flow-underwriting-driven business (formed by the 2022 merger of Shriram Transport and Shriram City Union Finance) that also does two-wheeler, gold and MSME loans.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Up from 8.48% a year ago and 8.19% the prior quarter — actually expanding, unlike most lenders on this dashboard. Note: this NBFC does headline NIM clearly, unlike Bajaj Finance and Cholamandalam — disclosure varies by company, not by bank-vs-NBFC as a rule.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Reported as 'Gross Stage 3 assets' (Ind-AS basis) — improved YoY and sequentially.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Reported as 'Net Stage 3 assets' — also improved.

cost_of_borrowing_pct.label: Cost of Borrowing
cost_of_borrowing_pct.note: Overall cost of liabilities; new incremental borrowing was cheaper at 7.73% this quarter on recent credit-rating upgrades.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: As of 30 Sep 2025 (closest available) — a pending ₹39,618 crore MUFG Bank equity infusion hadn't yet been reflected at quarter-end.

loan_book_growth.label: AUM Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in Assets Under Management.

bull: NIM actually expanded to 8.58%, up from 8.48% a year ago — a rare case of margin growth while most lenders on this dashboard are seeing compression.

Asset quality keeps improving (Gross Stage 3 4.54%, Net Stage 3 2.38%, both down YoY and sequentially) despite serving a structurally riskier customer base — used commercial vehicles, MSME, rural borrowers — than almost any other lender here.
A pending ₹39,618 crore equity infusion from MUFG Bank (Japan's largest bank) would materially strengthen the capital base and is a strong external vote of confidence in the franchise.
bear: Reported profit fell 22% YoY this quarter — almost entirely because last year's quarter included a one-off gain from selling its housing-finance subsidiary; core profit (excluding that) actually grew over 21%, but the headline number alone looks weak.
Cost of liabilities (8.69%) is the highest of any NBFC on this dashboard — a structurally more expensive funding base that comes with serving a riskier, more rural/retail borrower profile.
Heavily tied to India's commercial-vehicle cycle and rural/semi-urban incomes — a freight slowdown or weak monsoon flows through to this book faster than it would for a diversified bank.

value_chain.position: No physical chain — funding cost versus lending yield. Shriram is India's largest used commercial-vehicle financier, serving a structurally riskier, more rural and self-employed borrower base most banks avoid — high yield, high funding cost, deep cycle exposure.

tech_geo_tags.label: Used-CV and freight-cycle dependence: earnings track the used commercial-vehicle and freight cycle and rural incomes — a more volatile base than a diversified lender
A pending ~39,618-crore equity infusion from Japan's MUFG Bank brings major foreign capital and a strong external vote of confidence
Funding-cost sensitivity: the highest cost of borrowing among NBFCs tracked here, though recent rating upgrades have lowered incremental borrowing costs

SHRIRAMFIN.moat_note: India's largest player in used/pre-owned commercial vehicle financing, built on a four-decade-old distribution network (3,200+ branches, ~55% rural/semi-urban) and credit-assessment expertise in a segment most banks avoid — a genuine niche-scale moat.

BANKBARODA.name: Bank of Baroda
BANKBARODA.source_note: Bank of Baroda's own Q3 FY26 results and corroborating market coverage
BANKBARODA.business_core: Bank of Baroda is a government-owned (PSU) bank that takes deposits and lends across retail, agriculture, MSME and corporate India, with a meaningful overseas branch network left from its historically international footprint. Profitability is driven by its interest spread and continued recovery in asset quality after the sector-wide PSU-bank bad-loan cycle.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Global NIM (domestic alone was stronger, 2.93%). Lowest of any bank here, and management flags further compression below 2.75% as a key risk.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: The bank's lowest-ever Gross NPA ratio.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Provision coverage ratio of 92.73% sits behind this.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Lowest of any bank on this dashboard, and slipping from 39.33% a year ago.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Down from 16.54% the prior quarter, mostly on risk-weighted-asset growth — still comfortably above the regulatory minimum.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in global advances; RAM (Retail/Agri/MSME) now 61.8% of the book.

bull: Gross NPA fell to 2.04% — the lowest in the bank's history — backed by a 92.73% provision coverage ratio.
Loan growth accelerated to 14.65% YoY, with RAM (Retail/Agri/MSME) now 61.8% of advances — a real shift toward higher-quality, less concentrated lending.
Five-year net profit CAGR above 50% reflects a genuine post-recapitalisation turnaround, not a one-quarter blip.
bear: NIM kept compressing to 2.79% from 2.96% the prior quarter — the lowest of any bank on this dashboard, with management itself flagging further compression below 2.75% as a real risk.
Capital adequacy slid from 16.54% to 15.29% in a single quarter, mostly from risk-weighted-asset growth — still well above the regulatory floor, but the trend is worth tracking.
CASA ratio (38.45%) is the lowest of any bank here and continuing to slip — a structurally thinner cheap-funding cushion than the larger private banks.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. As a large government-owned bank, Bank of Baroda carries state policy priorities alongside commercial goals, and emerged bigger from India's PSU-bank consolidation (the Vijaya and Dena merger).

tech_geo_tags.label: Government majority ownership: policy and social-mandate obligations alongside commercial objectives
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR, the shared UPI dynamic
PSU-consolidation scale (post Vijaya and Dena merger) plus a cleaned-up balance sheet make it one of the stronger PSU franchises

BANKBARODA.moat_note: Scale as India's second-largest PSU bank + an extensive pan-India branch network + government-backed trust — the same structural advantages as SBI, at a smaller scale, with a credibly improving balance sheet after years of post-merger cleanup.

PNB.name: Punjab National Bank
PNB.source_note: PNB's own Q3 FY26 earnings call transcript and corroborating market coverage
PNB.business_core: Punjab National Bank is a large PSU bank (it absorbed Oriental Bank of Commerce and United Bank of India in 2020) that takes deposits and lends across retail, agriculture and MSME India. It has historically carried a heavier bad-loan legacy than private-bank peers, a position that has been steadily improving.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Global NIM, per management's own earnings-call figure (domestic alone was 2.65%). Down from 2.93% a year ago.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Down sharply from 4.09% a year ago and from 9.76% a few years before that.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Down from 0.41% a year ago, with provision coverage near 97%.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Among the lowest here, and still slipping.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Up 136 bps YoY — genuinely strengthening, not just stable.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in advances; retail lending alone grew 18.9%, led by vehicle loans (+35.7%).

bull: Net NPA fell to just 0.32% (from 0.41% a year ago), with provision coverage near 97% — among the strongest asset-quality positions of any PSU bank.
Capital adequacy improved meaningfully to 16.77%, up 136 bps from a year ago — a genuinely strengthening position, not just a stable one.
Retail loan growth was sharp (+18.9% YoY), led by vehicle loans (+35.7%) and housing (+14.5%) — a real shift toward higher-quality retail lending.
bear: NIM fell to 2.52% (global) from 2.93% a year ago — among the steepest margin compression of any bank on this dashboard, with management guiding for further near-term pressure before stabilising around FY27.
CASA ratio (37.1%) is among the lowest here, and continuing to slip — a structurally costlier funding base than top private banks.
This quarter's profit included a ₹700 crore one-off treasury gain from a stake sale — worth separating core banking profit from one-time gains when judging earnings quality.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. Around 70% government-owned. PNB's history includes the 2018 ~14,000-crore Nirav Modi fraud, after which its governance and operational-risk controls were rebuilt — a durable reminder that operational risk is a real line item for a bank this size and structure.

tech_geo_tags.label: Government majority ownership (~70%): policy and social mandates sit alongside commercial goals
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR, the shared UPI dynamic
Sharply improved asset quality and rising capital adequacy show the post-cleanup turnaround is real

PNB.moat_note: Over 130 years of legacy + a 10,000+ branch pan-India network + ~70% government ownership — classic PSU scale and trust, paired with an aggressive digital push (95%+ of transactions now digital).

FEDERALBNK.name: Federal Bank
FEDERALBNK.source_note: Federal Bank's own Q3 FY26 results and Screener.in's Q3 FY26 ratio summary
FEDERALBNK.business_core: Federal Bank is a Kerala-headquartered private bank with an unusually large share of India's inward NRI remittances flowing through its accounts, on top of standard retail, SME and corporate lending. It earns the interest spread plus fee income tied to that remittance and treasury franchise.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: The bank's best in a decade.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Up 191 bps YoY, with record fee income reducing reliance on interest income alone.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: A Blackstone capital infusion is expected to add ~50bps in Q4 FY26 alone, more through FY28.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: Slowest of any bank here — a deliberate choice, per management, to prioritise quality of growth over pace.

bull: Net NPA improved to 0.42% — the bank's best in a decade — with Gross NPA down to 1.72%, both moving the right direction for several straight quarters.
CASA ratio crossed 32% with record fee income (+19% YoY), genuine revenue diversification rather than just margin-chasing.

A landmark Blackstone capital infusion is expected to lift capital adequacy by roughly 50bps in Q4 FY26 alone, with more capital still arriving through FY28 — a multi-year capital tailwind most banks here don't have lined up.
bear: Loan growth (under 11% YoY) is the slowest of any bank on this dashboard — management is deliberately prioritising 'quality of growth' over pace, which is defensible, but it does mean ceding scale gains to faster-growing peers right now.

Return on equity (12.9% over the last 3 years per Screener) trails most peers here — a structurally lower profitability ceiling unless the Blackstone capital and mix-shift translate into materially higher returns.

Contingent liabilities are large in absolute terms (₹2.17 lakh crore) — standard for a bank this size, but worth understanding what's inside that figure rather than treating it as a red flag on its own.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. Federal's distinctive exposure is a century-old NRI and Kerala-diaspora deposit franchise, giving it unusual sensitivity to global-Indian remittance flows and currency movements (Gulf economies, USD/INR).

tech_geo_tags.label: Diaspora and remittance exposure: a large NRI deposit base ties Federal to Gulf-economy health and USD/INR moves more than a typical domestic bank
A large strategic capital partner (Blackstone) and a heavy fintech-partnership model extend distribution well beyond its Kerala base
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR — the same UPI dynamic facing all banks

FEDERALBNK.moat_note: A century-old, deeply entrenched franchise among India's NRI/Kerala diaspora (an unusually sticky, high-quality deposit base) + a newly secured large strategic capital partner (Blackstone) positioning it toward a top-5 private-bank ambition.

IDFCFIRSTB.name: IDFC First Bank
IDFCFIRSTB.source_note: IDFC First Bank's own Q3 FY26 press release, investor presentation, and earnings-call transcript
IDFCFIRSTB.business_core: IDFC First Bank is a young private bank (formed from the 2018 merger of IDFC Bank and Capital First) that built a mostly retail loan book from scratch — home loans, vehicle loans, MSME credit and a fast-growing credit-card business — while still working through legacy corporate/infrastructure-project loans inherited from IDFC's earlier project-finance roots.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Highest of any bank here — but the bank itself reports this 'gross of IBPC & sell-down,' a different methodology than most peers use, so treat the ranking directionally, not as a perfectly apples-to-apples figure.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Improved from 1.86% the prior quarter and 1.94% a year ago.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Ticked up slightly from 0.52% a year ago, even as Gross NPA improved.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Highest of any bank on this dashboard, up 390 bps YoY.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Includes the effect of ₹7,500 crore in CCPS converting to equity this quarter.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: Fastest of any bank here; 89% of the growth came from mortgages, vehicle loans, consumer loans, business banking and wholesale — a diversified mix.

bull: NIM (5.76%) and CASA (51.64%) are both the highest of any bank on this dashboard by a wide margin — a genuinely differentiated funding and margin profile for a bank still under 8 years old in its current form.
Loan book grew 20.93% YoY, the fastest here, with 89% of that growth spread across mortgages, vehicle loans, consumer loans, business banking and wholesale — a diversified mix, not one risky segment.
Asset quality kept improving even through rapid growth: Gross NPA fell to 1.69%, with MFI-related slippages specifically called out as improving.
bear: NIM is reported 'gross of IBPC & sell-down' — a different methodology than most banks use, so this figure isn't perfectly apples-to-apples with other banks' NIM on this dashboard.
Net NPA ticked up slightly (0.53% from 0.52%) even as Gross NPA improved — a small but real divergence worth watching given past stress in its MFI/unsecured book.

Return on assets, by management's own admission on the earnings call, remains low (around 0.5%) — a bank still proving out the profitability of its post-merger retail transformation, not yet a finished story.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. IDFC First's specific exposure is a meaningful microfinance and unsecured book at a time of sector-wide MFI stress — even as its purpose-built digital stack and high CASA give it a strong funding base to weather it.

tech_geo_tags.label: Microfinance and unsecured exposure: sector-wide MFI stress (NBFC-MFI GNPA doubled to ~4.1% by March 2025, expected to peak by March 2026) touches its book directly — it flagged MFI slippages improving, but the cycle is not over
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR, the shared UPI dynamic
A purpose-built digital stack and the highest CASA among banks tracked here give it a low-cost funding base to absorb the cycle

IDFCFIRSTB.moat_note: A purpose-built, fintech-grade digital banking experience (built fresh after the 2018 IDFC Bank–Capital First merger, not retrofitted onto a legacy core) + the fastest CASA-funded growth among private banks tracked here — still a relatively young, unproven-through-a-full-credit-cycle franchise compared to century-old peers.

BANDHANBNK.name: Bandhan Bank
BANDHANBNK.source_note: Bandhan Bank's own Q3 FY26 press release and corroborating market coverage
BANDHANBNK.business_core: Bandhan Bank grew out of India's largest microfinance institution, so a meaningful share of its book is still unsecured, group-liability microfinance lending to low-income borrowers — higher-yield but higher-risk — alongside a growing secured retail and housing-finance business it's been building to diversify.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Highest on this dashboard — but read this as compensation for high-risk microfinance lending, not superior banking economics. It comes paired with the lowest CASA ratio here and one of the higher Gross NPA ratios.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Down sharply from 5.02% just one quarter earlier — a 169bps improvement in a single quarter.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Down from 1.37% the prior quarter, with provision coverage at 70.8-84.3% depending on whether technical write-offs are included.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Lowest of any bank on this dashboard, down from 31.7% a year ago — fast-eroding cheap-deposit base.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Tier-1 alone was 16.5% — comfortably above regulatory requirements.

bull: Gross NPA fell sharply to 3.33% from 5.02% just one quarter earlier — a remarkable 169bps improvement suggesting the worst of its microfinance stress cycle may be behind it.
Profit rebounded 84% sequentially on materially better collections, with microfinance (EEB) collection efficiency holding strong at 98.2%.
Capital adequacy of 17.33% remains comfortably above regulatory requirements, leaving room to keep growing without needing fresh capital soon.
bear: That headline 5.9% NIM is the highest on this dashboard, but it reflects high-yield, high-risk microfinance lending, not efficient banking — it comes paired with the lowest CASA ratio here (27.3%) and one of the higher Gross NPA ratios (3.33%). Read it as 'compensation for risk taken,' not 'best margins.'
CASA ratio kept sliding — 31.7% a year ago to 28.0% to 27.3% now — the lowest and fastest-eroding cheap-deposit base of any bank tracked here.

Profit is still down 51.7% YoY despite the sequential rebound, and the bank sold ₹6,872 crore of bad microfinance loans to asset reconstruction companies in December 2025 — a sign the cleanup is real, but also that there was a lot to clean up.

value_chain.position: No physical chain, but Bandhan's model is built on microfinance (its EEB book), so it sits at the sharp end of the sector-wide MFI stress cycle — and its footprint is also geographically concentrated in East India.

tech_geo_tags.label: Microfinance stress cycle: NBFC-MFI GNPA doubled to ~4.1% by March 2025 and is expected to peak by March 2026; Bandhan already sold ~6,872 crore of bad MFI loans (Dec 2025) and saw its own GNPA swing sharply — a direct read on this cycle
Geographic concentration: a heavy East-India footprint amplifies exposure to regional income shocks and climate-linked rural volatility
RBI cut the microfinance consumer-credit risk weight from 125% back to 100% (March 2025), and collection efficiency is recovering — early signs the worst of the cycle may be passing

BANDHANBNK.moat_note: Deep, two-decade-old rural/semi-urban microfinance distribution and credit-assessment expertise — a genuine niche moat in a segment most universal banks avoid, though it carries structurally higher volatility through credit cycles than a diversified bank.

AUBANK.name: AU Small Finance Bank
AUBANK.source_note: AU Small Finance Bank's own Q3 FY26 results and corroborating market coverage
AUBANK.business_core: AU Small Finance Bank converted from a vehicle-finance NBFC into a small finance bank in 2017. It lends heavily to underbanked retail and MSME customers in semi-urban and rural Rajasthan and neighbouring states — vehicle and MSME loans originally, now alongside a fuller banking suite of deposits and cards.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Down from 5.9% a year ago, as deposit competition stays intense.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: Improved from 2.41% the prior quarter; stable for three straight quarters.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Unchanged for three consecutive quarters — a sign of consistent, not volatile, credit performance.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: Well above regulatory requirements, supporting continued growth.

loan_book_growth.label: Loan Book Growth
loan_book_growth.note: YoY growth in gross loan portfolio.

bull: Net profit jumped 26% YoY with provisions down 34% YoY even as the loan book grew a healthy 19.3% — growth and credit discipline improving together, not at each other's expense.

Received in-principle RBI approval to transition into a Universal Bank — a structural unlock that should lower funding costs over time and let it compete more directly with full-service private banks.
Asset quality has been stable for three straight quarters (GNPA 2.30%, NNPA 0.88% unchanged), weathering the small-finance-bank sector's recent microfinance stress better than several peers.
bear: NIM kept compressing — 5.7%, down from 5.9% a year ago — as competition for deposits stays intense; management itself called the deposit environment 'highly competitive.'
CASA ratio slipped from 31.0% a year ago to 29.0% now — the same cheap-deposit erosion playing out across nearly every bank on this dashboard, not unique to AU SFB.
At least one tracker has flagged the valuation as 'very expensive' with a PEG ratio above 2 — a reminder that strong execution doesn't automatically mean a cheap stock.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. AU's specific exposure is the small-finance-bank sector's microfinance stress plus an intense deposit-competition environment — set against a structural upgrade, an in-principle RBI approval to become a Universal Bank.

tech_geo_tags.label: Universal Bank transition: in-principle RBI approval to convert from an SFB should lower funding costs and widen its addressable market over time
Microfinance and unsecured stress: as an SFB with meaningful unsecured exposure, AU sits in the segment RBI flagged as FY26's most stressed (unsecured personal loans, microfinance, SME)
Deposit-competition intensity: management itself calls the deposit environment highly competitive — NIM and CASA are under the same pressure as the whole sector

AUBANK.moat_note: A digital-first, tech-forward small finance bank with the broadest branch network among SFBs (2,700+ touchpoints across 21 states) + a fresh in-principle approval to become a Universal Bank, a structural upgrade most SFB peers don't yet have in hand.

CANBK.name: Canara Bank
CANBK.source_note: Canara Bank's own Q3 FY26 results, Screener.in's ratio summary, and India Ratings commentary
CANBK.business_core: Canara Bank is a PSU bank with a large branch network concentrated in South India, taking deposits and lending across retail, agriculture, MSME and corporate customers. Like other PSU banks, its profitability is tied to the interest spread and to continued recovery in legacy asset quality.

nim.label: Net Interest Margin
nim.note: Down from 2.80% for all of FY25 — management is guiding to recover toward 2.75-2.80% during FY26.

gnpa_pct.label: Gross NPA
gnpa_pct.note: A record low for the bank, down from 3.34% a year ago.

nnpa_pct.label: Net NPA
nnpa_pct.note: Down from 0.89% a year ago, with provision coverage above 94%.

casa_pct.label: CASA Ratio
casa_pct.note: Among the lower end of this dashboard; management's own target is to recover to just 32%, still below its 32.4% level two years ago.

car_pct.label: Capital Adequacy
car_pct.note: CET1 alone is lower (12.37%) than most PSU peers with similar asset quality — the headline figure leans more on AT1/Tier-2 instruments.

bull: Gross NPA hit a record low of 2.08% (from 3.34% a year ago) with Net NPA at just 0.45% and provision coverage above 94% — asset quality now genuinely rivals the better private banks, not just other PSU peers.
Profit grew 25.6% YoY for the seventh straight quarter of 18%+ YoY growth — a sustained trend, not a one-off beat.
Raised ₹3,500 crore via AT1 bonds during the quarter, reinforcing an already-comfortable 16.50% capital adequacy ratio with room to keep growing.
bear: A meaningful share of this quarter's profit growth came from a one-time ~₹1,800 crore gain on reclassifying two subsidiaries (Canara Robeco AMC, Canara HSBC Life) as associates — worth separating from core lending performance when judging earnings quality.

NIM kept falling to 2.50% from 2.80% across FY25, and CASA (29.52%) is among the lower end of this dashboard, with management's own near-term target being just 32% — still below its level two years ago.

CET1 — the purest measure of core capital — at 12.37% is lower than most PSU peers with comparable asset quality, per India Ratings; the comfortable headline capital ratio leans more on AT1/Tier-2 instruments than some peers.

value_chain.position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. Government-owned, and one of India's largest PSU banks with a lead-bank role coordinating banking across many states and districts — deep, sovereign-anchored distribution.

tech_geo_tags.label: Government majority ownership plus lead-bank role: coordinates priority-sector and inclusion banking across states, a mandate beyond pure commercial lending
Platform disintermediation plus zero-MDR, the shared UPI dynamic
Record-low NPAs and strong profit growth show a genuine turnaround, though core CET1 capital is thinner than some peers

CANBK.moat_note: Scale as India's fourth-largest PSU bank and sixth-largest bank overall (10,000+ domestic branches), ranked #4 as lead bank across India's states/UTs/districts — deep, sovereign-anchored distribution that's nearly impossible for a private bank to replicate quickly.

HINDUNILVR.name: Hindustan Unilever
HINDUNILVR.source_note: HUL's own Q3 FY26 earnings call (Feb 12 2026), Business Standard, Axis Direct research note, Screener.in (FY26 annual), MarketsMojo — six sources cross-checked
HINDUNILVR.business_core: Hindustan Unilever (Unilever's Indian arm) is India's largest FMCG company: it manufactures and sells everyday packaged goods — soaps, detergents, shampoos, packaged foods, skincare — under brands like Lux, Surf Excel and Dove, distributed through India's largest retail reach network. Margins depend on brand pricing power versus input costs (palm oil and other commodity derivatives).

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Continuing operations only, after the Dec 2025 ice cream (Kwality Wall's) demerger. Underlying Sales Growth (USG) was 5%.

volume_growth_pct.label: Volume Growth (UVG)
volume_growth_pct.note: Underlying Volume Growth — HUL's own preferred demand measure. Management called this the highest UVG in the last 12 quarters.

gross_margin_pct.label: Gross Margin
gross_margin_pct.note: Down 28 bps YoY (Axis Direct). Key headwind: palm oil and crude-linked raw material inflation.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Down 70 bps YoY. Within HUL's own guided range of 22–23%; management reaffirmed the range for FY26.

roce_pct.label: ROCE
roce_pct.note: Return on Capital Employed for FY26 (trailing). Zero long-term debt means essentially all capital is equity — the ROCE reflects genuine business quality, not financial leverage.

anp_spend_pct.label: A&P Spend % Revenue
anp_spend_pct.note: ₹1,522 crore on advertising & promotion in Q3 FY26. Full-year FY26 was 9.8% of turnover. Display-only metric — higher is brand investment, not waste; lower isn't automatically better.

bull: Volume growth (4% UVG) is the highest in 12 quarters, and management described demand trends as showing 'early signs of recovery' on the Q3 earnings call — a genuine turning point after several quarters of soft volumes, not just narrative.
ROCE of 31.61% and zero long-term debt — this company creates substantial returns on capital without leverage; that's a quality of earnings test most companies fail.

The portfolio rotation is real: ice cream (low-margin) is now demerged and separately listed; premium digital-native brands Minimalist and OZiva are fully owned; quick-commerce now ~3% of sales and doubling every quarter.
bear: The reported 121% profit surge is almost entirely a one-time accounting gain (₹4,485 crore exceptional item from the ice cream demerger) — adjusted PAT from continuing operations grew about 1% YoY, which is the real underlying number.

EBITDA margins compressed 70 bps YoY to 23.3% — palm oil and crude-linked raw material costs are the named culprit; management guided to staying within 22–23%, not expanding back to historical 24–25%.

Revenue growth of 5.7% (and 5-year sales CAGR of just ~6.5%) is unspectacular for a company trading at over 49x FY27E earnings per analyst estimates — the valuation assumes flawless execution on premiumisation and quick-commerce scaling, with little room for a miss.

value_chain.position: Downstream brand owner and manufacturer — HUL doesn't grow its own raw materials, it captures value through 20 'billion-rupee' brands and a 9-million-outlet distribution network that turns commodity inputs into premium-priced finished goods.
value_chain.note: Import-dependent on palm oil and crude-linked raw materials, named directly as the Q3 gross-margin headwind — a live, current example of the import-exposure pattern, not a hypothetical.

tech_geo_tags.label: Distribution disintermediation: quick-commerce now ~3% of sales and doubling each quarter — reshaping who controls shelf access and at what margin, a genuinely new risk this category didn't face five years ago
Commodity/import exposure: palm oil and crude-linked raw materials, directly cited as the quarter's gross-margin pressure point
Premium digital-native brand acquisitions (Minimalist, OZiva) give HUL its own foothold in the same disruptive channel rather than only defending against it

HINDUNILVR.moat_note: 20 'billion-rupee brands' including Dove, Surf Excel, Lux, Lipton, Horlicks, and Lakmé — a brand portfolio moat paired with a 9-million-outlet distribution network built over 90+ years. The moat is real; the question is whether the premium valuation already prices it in fully.

ITC.name: ITC Limited
ITC.source_note: ITC's own Q3 FY26 investor presentation (itcportal.com), Motilal Oswal research note, Business Standard, Tobacco Insider, MarketsMojo, Screener.in — seven sources cross-checked
ITC.business_core: ITC is a diversified conglomerate whose historically dominant, highest-margin business is cigarettes (Gold Flake and other brands) — a reliable cash generator that funds a fast-growing FMCG-foods and personal-care portfolio (Aashirvaad, Sunfeast, Bingo, Savlon), plus hotels, paperboard/packaging and agri-trading businesses. Its profit mix remains cigarette-heavy even as management pushes to diversify.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Consolidated gross revenue +7.1% per ITC's own presentation; net-of-excise revenue used here (+6.7%). Cigarettes excise duty flows through gross revenue — both bases are valid, but this is the net figure for comparability.

volume_growth_pct.label: Volume Growth
volume_growth_pct.note: Cigarette segment volume growth, ITC's single largest segment by revenue and profit. FMCG-Others (packaged foods, personal care) revenues grew +11.1% YoY — no single volume % disclosed for the combined company.

gross_margin_pct.label: Gross Margin
gross_margin_pct.note: Not directly comparable to HUL. ITC's gross revenue includes cigarette excise duty (a pass-through tax), which inflates the revenue denominator and makes computed gross margin meaningfully different in kind from a pure-FMCG company's. Shown as — rather than presenting a misleading number.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Standalone EBITDA margin, up 50 bps YoY to 35.1%. Higher than HUL's 23.3%, but driven primarily by the structurally higher-margin cigarette business — not a like-for-like comparison.

roce_pct.label: ROCE
roce_pct.note: Highest of any company on this dashboard. Reflects the high-margin, capital-light cigarette franchise. ROE was 27.82%.

anp_spend_pct.label: A&P Spend % Revenue
anp_spend_pct.note: Not disclosed as a combined % of net revenue for Q3. ITC reports advertising and promotion costs selectively at segment level. Shown as — rather than estimated.

bull: ROCE of 44.26% is the highest of any company on this dashboard — a direct result of the cigarette business's structurally high margins and low capital requirements. The business model itself generates extraordinary returns on the capital it actually deploys.

FMCG-Others (non-cigarette packaged foods, personal care, agarbattis) grew +11.1% revenue YoY with EBITDA margins expanding 145 bps — the diversification away from cigarettes is actually delivering results, not just a stated ambition.
Dividend yield of ~2% is the highest among FMCG peers tracked here, backed by consistent FCF from the cigarette franchise. Management declared ₹6.50/share interim dividend this quarter.
bear: A cigarette excise duty hike effective February 1, 2026 (₹2.05–₹8.50 per stick by length, replacing compensation cess) came just after Q3 ended — so this quarter's results don't reflect it yet. The stock fell ~20% from its 52-week high near ₹471 to ₹303–318 around results, pricing in the regulatory risk the numbers haven't yet shown.
Cigarette EBIT margins already contracted 140 bps YoY to 56.7% even before the excise hike, on rising leaf tobacco costs — margin pressure was already building in the core business.

~70–75% of total profit still comes from cigarettes: a business facing annual regulatory risk, secular volume headwinds from health awareness, and an illicit trade market that grows every time legal prices are raised. The entire conglomerate's financial health is structurally dependent on one regulated, shrinking-by-design category.

value_chain.position: Unusually vertically integrated for an FMCG company — ITC sources leaf tobacco directly through its own Agri Business arm, manufactures, brands, and distributes through a network giving it ~80% share of the organized cigarette market, capturing value at nearly every stage rather than depending on outside suppliers.

tech_geo_tags.label: Regulatory/tax exposure: a cigarette excise duty hike (₹2.05-8.50 per stick) took effect Feb 1, 2026, just after this quarter closed — the single largest near-term risk to the value chain this profit depends on most
Illicit trade risk: every legal price increase historically expands the untaxed market, eroding the volume base the legal, taxed supply chain depends on

FMCG-Others diversification (+11.1% revenue growth this quarter) is a genuine, working hedge that reduces dependence on the cigarette value chain over time, even if it's not yet large enough to offset it

ITC.moat_note: ~80% share of India's organized domestic cigarette market (entrenched brands, unique distribution, high switching friction) + a genuinely growing FMCG portfolio (Sunfeast, Bingo, Fiama, Classmate) + a valuable hotel and real estate portfolio. The core moat is durable; the regulatory and ESG risk discount on it is the ongoing debate.

TCS.name: Tata Consultancy Services
TCS.source_note: TCS's own Q3 FY26 press release, fact sheet, and earnings-call transcript (tcs.com)
TCS.business_core: Tata Consultancy Services sells software development, IT consulting and outsourced technology operations to large global enterprises, billed largely on consultant time or fixed-price/outcome contracts. Because revenue is fundamentally billed hours and project fees, headcount, utilisation rates and pricing per hour drive margins — and it now discloses a distinct, growing AI-services revenue line.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Reported INR terms. Constant-currency revenue actually fell 2.6% YoY — the gap is currency translation, not real growth. Worth reading both numbers together.

operating_margin_pct.label: Operating Margin
operating_margin_pct.note: Held steady YoY despite wage hikes — margin discipline maintained through a soft-growth environment.

attrition_pct.label: Attrition (LTM)
attrition_pct.note: Voluntary attrition, trailing twelve months, IT services.

deal_tcv_usd_bn.label: Deal TCV (Quarter)
deal_tcv_usd_bn.note: Total Contract Value signed this quarter, including one mega deal in North America. A forward-looking demand signal, not revenue already booked.

roce_pct.label: ROCE
roce_pct.note: Not separately headlined by TCS in its own Q3 disclosures — shown as — rather than estimated.

ai_revenue_growth_pct.label: AI Revenue Growth (QoQ)
ai_revenue_growth_pct.note: Annualised AI services revenue reached $1.8bn, up 17.3% sequentially in constant currency. Display-only: this is a new, inconsistently-disclosed metric across the sector — see the bear case for why it can't yet be ranked fairly against peers.

bull: AI is becoming a real, measurable revenue line, not just a talking point: annualised AI services revenue hit $1.8 billion, growing 17.3% sequentially — management's stated ambition is to become 'the world's largest AI-led technology services company.'

Deal TCV of $9.3 billion this quarter (with a North America mega deal) gives strong revenue visibility — a forward indicator that current bookings exceed current billing, a healthy sign for future quarters.
Operating margin held steady at 25.2% despite wage hikes — disciplined cost management even while growth stayed modest.
bear: Constant-currency revenue actually declined 2.6% YoY — the headline 4.9% INR growth is mostly currency translation, not real organic demand. This is the structural GenAI-era story the project plan flagged: pricing and volume pressure in traditional delivery work, even at the sector's largest, most efficient player.

Reported net profit fell YoY, driven by exceptional items this quarter — management says this doesn't reflect core business weakness, but it's worth separating headline profit from underlying trend regardless.

AI revenue, while growing fast, is still a small fraction of TCS's ~$30bn annual revenue base — the disclosed dollar figure ($1.8bn annualised) needs context against total scale, not just its growth rate, before treating it as a turnaround signal.

value_chain.position: Downstream IT service provider — there's no physical raw-material chain here; the enterprise client sets project scope and retains the anchor position, while TCS competes on delivery quality, cost, and increasingly AI-augmented productivity within that scope.
value_chain.note: Revenue is broadly diversified across BFSI (~28%), manufacturing, energy/utilities, and retail — no single client or vertical disclosed as a concentration risk.

tech_geo_tags.label: AI/automation exposure: structural threat to the traditional staffing-pyramid billing model — this quarter's constant-currency revenue actually fell 2.6% YoY even as reported INR revenue grew, the live evidence of pricing/volume pressure
AI/automation exposure: also a measurable tailwind — $1.8bn annualised AI-native revenue, +17.3% QoQ, the fastest-growing disclosed line in the business
Currency exposure: the bulk of revenue is billed in USD/GBP/EUR — a weaker rupee flatters reported INR growth even when underlying demand (constant-currency) is flat or falling

TCS.moat_note: Scale (582,000+ employees, 202 delivery centers, 55 countries) + five decades of enterprise relationships + a Tata Group balance sheet that can absorb AI-transition investment most competitors can't. The open question — explicit in the project plan's value-chain analysis — is whether scale remains a moat or becomes a cost disadvantage as AI compresses the traditional staffing-pyramid economics this scale was built around.

INFY.name: Infosys
INFY.source_note: Infosys's own Q3 FY26 SEC Form 6-K press release and fact sheet, plus corroborating market coverage (Zee Business, Republic World)
INFY.business_core: Infosys runs the same core IT-services business model as TCS — global enterprise clients pay for software development, consulting and outsourced technology operations — with margins driven by utilisation, the offshore/onsite delivery mix and wage inflation. Unlike TCS, it does not disclose a separate standalone AI-revenue figure.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Reported INR terms, per Infosys's own fact sheet. Constant-currency growth was just 1.7% — similar currency-vs-real gap to TCS, same sector-wide pattern.

operating_margin_pct.label: Operating Margin
operating_margin_pct.note: Reported IFRS basis. Adjusted (excluding a one-time Labour Codes charge) was higher at 21.2%, sequentially up 0.2pp — the cleaner read of underlying margin trend.

attrition_pct.label: Attrition (LTM)
attrition_pct.note: Down from 14.3% the prior quarter and 13.7% a year ago — the lowest of the two IT companies tracked here, and genuinely improving, not just stable.

deal_tcv_usd_bn.label: Deal TCV (Quarter)
deal_tcv_usd_bn.note: Total Contract Value of large deal wins this quarter, with 57% net new (i.e., not just renewals of existing work).

roce_pct.label: ROCE
roce_pct.note: Not separately headlined in Q3 disclosures (ROE was cited at 32.8% in the earnings call, a related but different measure) — shown as — rather than conflating the two.

ai_revenue_growth_pct.label: AI Revenue Growth (QoQ)
ai_revenue_growth_pct.note: Infosys discloses AI traction qualitatively (via its 'Topaz' platform) and ties it to deal wins and market-share commentary, but doesn't publish a standalone AI-revenue growth % the way TCS does. Shown as — rather than guessed — itself a useful data point: AI-revenue disclosure isn't yet standardised across the sector.

bull: Attrition fell to 12.3% LTM, down from 14.3% the prior quarter — a real, improving trend, and the lowest of the two IT companies tracked here, suggesting better employee retention even amid sector-wide AI-driven uncertainty.
Management raised FY26 revenue guidance (to 3.0-3.5% constant currency) rather than cutting it — a vote of confidence from the company closest to its own numbers, not just analyst optimism.
Deal TCV of $4.8bn with 57% net new business signals real incremental demand, not just contract renewals — a healthier mix than pure renewal-driven bookings.
bear: Constant-currency revenue growth of just 1.7% YoY is barely positive — the same structural pattern as TCS, the clearest evidence yet that this is a sector-wide GenAI-era growth slowdown, not one company's execution problem.

Reported operating margin (18.4%) was meaningfully below the adjusted figure (21.2%) due to a one-time Labour Codes charge — worth using the adjusted number for trend analysis, but the gap itself shows how much one-off items can swing a single quarter's headline margin.

Unlike TCS, Infosys doesn't publish a standalone AI-revenue growth metric — making it genuinely harder to judge whether its AI investments (branded 'Topaz') are translating into revenue at a comparable pace, or whether the absence of a disclosed number is itself information.

value_chain.position: Downstream IT service provider — the same structural position as TCS: no physical chain, the enterprise client sets scope, and Infosys competes on consulting-led delivery and, increasingly, on enterprise AI integration through its Topaz platform.
value_chain.note: Revenue concentration by segment is broadly similar to sector peers (financial services, manufacturing, retail) — no single client disclosed as a standout concentration risk.

tech_geo_tags.label: AI/automation exposure: structural threat to the traditional billing model — constant-currency revenue growth of just 1.7% YoY this quarter, barely positive, the same sector-wide pattern visible at TCS

AI/automation exposure: Topaz platform is management's stated growth strategy, tied to raised FY26 guidance — but without a disclosed revenue figure, it's a qualitative tailwind for now, not yet a quantified one
Currency exposure: similar USD-heavy revenue mix to TCS — the reported-vs-constant-currency growth gap (3.2% vs 1.7%) reflects rupee movement more than real demand change

INFY.moat_note: Deep enterprise AI partnerships (Topaz platform) + consulting-led client relationships across BFSI, manufacturing, and retail + one of the industry's strongest cash-generation track records (consistently 100%+ FCF-to-net-profit conversion). Like TCS, the moat built on engineering headcount scale is the exact thing GenAI is putting the most pressure on — the project plan's flagged sector risk, not a hypothetical one.

SUNPHARMA.name: Sun Pharmaceutical Industries
SUNPHARMA.source_note: Sun Pharma's own Q3 FY26 press release and earnings call transcript (sunpharma.com), cross-checked against Business Standard and AlphaStreet
SUNPHARMA.business_core: Sun Pharmaceutical Industries, India's largest drugmaker, sells both low-margin, volume-driven generic drugs (mainly in the US and India) and increasingly higher-margin branded specialty medicines — particularly dermatology — sold mostly in the US under its Innovative Medicines franchise. Recent profit growth has come from this shift toward specialty products, not just generic volumes.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Includes a $55 million one-time milestone payment within Global Innovative Medicines — underlying growth ex-milestone was still strong but slightly lower.

gross_margin_pct.label: Gross Margin
gross_margin_pct.note: Up YoY on a better product mix — reflects the growing share of higher-margin specialty/branded business.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Up 260bps YoY — rare to see margin expand alongside double-digit revenue growth in pharma this quarter.

us_business_growth_pct.label: US Business Growth
us_business_growth_pct.note: Flat — Innovative Medicines growth offset by generic price erosion. The same US pricing pressure hitting the whole sector, just better-hedged here by the specialty mix.

rd_pct_sales.label: R&D % of Sales
rd_pct_sales.note: ₹892.8 crore this quarter. Display-only: higher R&D is pipeline investment, not unambiguously 'better' without knowing what it funds.

anda_pending_approval_pipeline.label: ANDAs Pending FDA Approval
anda_pending_approval_pipeline.note: 550 already approved in the US; 116 pending (28 with tentative approval). Display-only — pipeline depth, not a ranked quality measure.

bull: Revenue grew 15.1% YoY while EBITDA margin simultaneously expanded 260bps to 31.9% — growth and margin expansion together is the harder combination to pull off, and most pharma peers this quarter are managing only one or the other.

Global Innovative Medicines (the higher-margin, less price-competitive segment) grew 13.2% ex-milestone and now makes up 21.2% of sales — a real, working strategic shift away from commodity generics pricing pressure, not just a stated ambition.
Maintains India's #1 pharma position with market share still increasing (8.4%), plus a fortress balance sheet ($3.2 billion net cash) to keep funding the pipeline without urgency.
bear: US business was flat (+0.6%) — Innovative Medicines growth was offset by generic price erosion, the same structural US pricing pressure hitting the whole Indian pharma export sector. Better-hedged here, not immune.

An exceptional charge of ₹489.5 crore this quarter (wage-code gratuity + a legal settlement provision) means the headline 16% profit growth somewhat overstates underlying performance — adjusted net profit grew a more modest 9.9%.
116 ANDAs remain pending FDA approval — normal pipeline depth, but every pending filing carries unpredictable regulatory-timeline risk that can delay expected launches by quarters, sometimes years.

value_chain.position: Vertically integrated specialty/generics manufacturer — API production mostly feeds its own formulation business internally (external API sales are tiny, under 4% of revenue), with an increasingly important Innovative/Specialty Medicines arm sold directly into the regulated US market.
value_chain.note: Manufacturing facilities span five continents serving 100+ countries — geographically diversified supply, not concentrated in one country's regulatory or trade-policy risk the way some peers are.

tech_geo_tags.label: US regulatory exposure: 116 ANDAs pending FDA approval — normal pipeline, but each carries unpredictable inspection/approval-timeline risk standard to the sector
US generic pricing pressure: flat US business this quarter as Innovative Medicines gains were offset by generic price erosion — a sector-wide structural headwind, not company-specific
Mix-shift tailwind: Innovative/Specialty Medicines now 21%+ of sales and growing faster than the overall business — a genuine, working hedge against commodity generic pricing pressure

SUNPHARMA.moat_note: World's largest specialty generics company + India's #1 pharma franchise by both market share and prescription volume + a genuinely differentiated innovative pipeline (5 novel entities in clinical stage) smaller peers can't easily replicate — though even this scale doesn't fully insulate the legacy US generics book from sector-wide pricing pressure.

DRREDDY.name: Dr. Reddy's Laboratories
DRREDDY.source_note: Dr. Reddy's own Q3 FY26 SEC Form 6-K filing and earnings call transcript, cross-checked against Investing.com and AlphaSpread
DRREDDY.business_core: Dr. Reddy's Laboratories sells generic drugs globally (US, Russia/CIS, India, Europe) alongside a smaller branded and biosimilars business. Revenue leans heavily on the US generics market, where pricing is volatile and one-off patent-cliff opportunities (like its Revlimid/Lenalidomide authorised-generic window) can swing a single product's contribution sharply from year to year.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Broad-based growth in India/Europe/Emerging Markets/PSAI was largely offset by a sharp North America decline (see bear case).

gross_margin_pct.label: Gross Margin
gross_margin_pct.note: Down from 58.7% a year earlier — a real, sizeable compression reflecting pricing pressure and product mix, not a rounding difference.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Down from 27.5% a year ago. 24.8% excluding a one-time Indian labour-code provision — still down meaningfully YoY even adjusted.

us_business_growth_pct.label: US Business Growth
us_business_growth_pct.note: North America declined specifically on falling Lenalidomide sales and price erosion — see the value chain note below for why this is structural, not random.

rd_pct_sales.label: R&D % of Sales
rd_pct_sales.note: Down from 8.0% a year ago (6.8% excluding a one-off), reflecting lower biosimilar development spend as Abatacept investment completes. Display-only.

anda_pending_approval_pipeline.label: ANDAs Pending FDA Approval
anda_pending_approval_pipeline.note: Per FY26 annual report: 75 pending, 43 of which are Paragraph IV filings (challenging existing patents — higher potential reward, also higher litigation risk). Display-only.

bull: India business grew a strong 19% YoY (organic growth over 17%), led by innovation brands, new launches, and the acquired Stugeron portfolio — proof the domestic franchise is healthy even as other markets wobble.

Actively pivoting toward biosimilars (trastuzumab, adalimumab in pipeline) and complex generics — a deliberate strategic shift away from the commodity small-molecule pricing pressure visible in this quarter's results.
Net cash surplus of ₹3,069 crore and positive free cash flow (₹374 crore) even in a tougher quarter — balance sheet strength to fund the pipeline pivot without urgency.
bear: North America revenue fell 16% YoY, driven specifically by declining Lenalidomide sales as the volume-capped settlement period (from the 2020 Celgene/BMS patent settlement) approaches its January 31, 2026 full-volume-unlock date — a single-molecule concentration risk now visibly playing out in the numbers, not a hypothetical risk anymore.

EBITDA margin compressed sharply to 23.5% from 27.5% a year ago — even the adjusted figure (24.8%, excluding a one-time labour-code provision) is still down nearly 300bps YoY, a real deterioration, not just a one-off accounting item.
Net profit fell 14% YoY and 16% sequentially — alongside revenue growth slowing to 4.4% and margin compression, three separate data points pointing the same direction this quarter.

value_chain.position: Integrated generics and emerging biosimilars player — develops products 'end-to-end across API and formulations' per its own FY26 annual report, with historically heavier reliance on a small number of high-value US generic opportunities than Sun Pharma's more diversified, specialty-tilted mix.
value_chain.note: 10 manufacturing facilities, 7 FDA-approved and 3 EMA-approved — a credible, internationally-inspected manufacturing base, not a single-site concentration risk.

tech_geo_tags.label: Single-molecule concentration risk realized in real time: North America revenue fell 16% YoY as the Lenalidomide volume-cap settlement (from 2020 Celgene/BMS litigation) approaches its January 2026 full-unlock date, intensifying competition on what had been a uniquely profitable, volume-limited opportunity

Paragraph IV pipeline risk/reward: 43 of 75 pending ANDAs are Paragraph IV filings (challenging existing patents) — higher potential reward (180-day exclusivity if first-to-file) but also real patent-litigation risk, as seen in the company's own recent Esperion/NEXLETOL settlement

Biosimilars pivot: active pipeline in oncology and autoimmune categories (trastuzumab, adalimumab) — a structural move toward higher-barrier-to-entry, higher-margin categories, away from commodity small-molecule generics

DRREDDY.moat_note: Genuine 'end-to-end' API-to-formulation development capability (a real vertical-integration moat, not just a slogan) + an early, credible biosimilars pipeline in oncology/autoimmune — historically more reliant than Sun Pharma on a handful of high-value US generic opportunities like Lenalidomide, which cuts both ways: outsized windfalls when they land, and now, visible pain as one of those windfalls fades on a schedule set years ago.

MARUTI.name: Maruti Suzuki India
MARUTI.source_note: Maruti Suzuki's own Q3 FY26 results press release (marutisuzuki.com), Business Standard, and Multibagg AI's ratio breakdown — cross-checked
MARUTI.business_core: Maruti Suzuki is India's largest passenger-vehicle maker (majority owned by Japan's Suzuki Motor Corp) — it designs, assembles and sells cars and SUVs (Swift, Baleno, Brezza, Fronx) mainly to Indian retail buyers through the country's largest dealer network, plus a fast-growing export business. Revenue comes from vehicle sales; margin is squeezed between what buyers will pay and the cost of steel, semiconductors and other components.

volume_growth_pct.label: Volume Growth
volume_growth_pct.note: Vehicles sold YoY — 667,769 units, its highest-ever quarterly sales.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Boosted by the Sept 2025 GST cut on small cars (28% → 18%) pulling forward demand.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Down from 13.8% a year ago — a one-time ₹593.9 crore Labour Code provision alone cost ~125 bps.

realization_growth_pct.label: Realization/Mix Growth
realization_growth_pct.note: Implied average price/mix growth (revenue growth minus volume growth) — a richer SUV-heavy mix, not just more units.

bull: Highest-ever quarterly volumes (667,769 units) and a record December, with dealer inventory lean at just 3 days — real demand, not a stocking illusion.
Exports are now roughly 45% of India's total PV exports, about double nearest rival Hyundai's share — a genuine structural export moat.
Zero long-term debt and a large net-cash position fund its ₹70,000 crore FY31 investment plan (8 new SUVs, EV/hybrid rollout) without straining the balance sheet.
bear: PV market share has slipped from around 50% in 2020 to below 40% now, as SUV-focused rivals (Mahindra, Tata, Hyundai) grow faster than the overall market.
EBITDA margin compressed 210 bps YoY on commodity costs, FX and a one-time labour-law provision — the GST-driven volume surge came at a real profitability cost.
Its first EV launched only in 2025, well behind Tata Motors and Mahindra, who already hold established EV market share — a late mover in the segment reshaping the industry.

value_chain.position: Maruti sits at the assembly end of the auto value chain — it designs vehicles with Suzuki's platform engineering and assembles them domestically, but depends on thousands of component suppliers for steel, electronics, semiconductors and, increasingly, rare-earth magnets used in hybrid/EV motors — a supply chain with real China exposure.
value_chain.note: Its domestic dealer and service network is Maruti's strongest moat layer — the widest reach of any Indian carmaker, hard for new entrants, including EV-first players, to replicate quickly.

tech_geo_tags.label: Rare-earth magnet supply: China's export curbs on rare-earth magnets (used in EV/hybrid motors) are a sector-wide supply risk that touches Maruti's hybrid and upcoming EV ramp-up directly
GST 2.0 tailwind: the Sept 2025 cut in small-car GST from 28% to 18% structurally lowered price points and pulled forward demand — a policy tailwind still working through the order book
BS-VII and stricter emission/safety norms on the horizon will require fresh R&D and capex across the industry, a cost sector-wide, not Maruti-specific

MARUTI.moat_note: Brand trust + India's largest dealer/service network + decades of cost-engineering discipline with Suzuki. Still India's largest carmaker by volume even as its share erodes.

TMPV.name: Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles
TMPV.source_note: Tata Motors Passenger Vehicles' own Q3 FY26 press release (cars.tatamotors.com), Storyboard18, and InvestyWise — cross-checked. Note: ticker/name changed in Oct-Nov 2025 demerger — see business_core.
