BAJAJFINSV.name: Bajaj Finserv
BAJAJFINSV.as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
BAJAJFINSV.source_note: Bajaj Finserv's own Q4 & FY26 results and investor presentation, cross-checked against MarketsMojo, Whalesbook and APAC Media coverage
BAJAJFINSV.business_core: Bajaj Finserv is the holding company of the Bajaj group's financial-services businesses — its value comes almost entirely from three large subsidiaries: Bajaj Finance (India's leading consumer-lending NBFC), Bajaj Allianz General Insurance and Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance (now renamed after Bajaj bought out Allianz's stake). Owning Bajaj Finserv is effectively owning a bundle of lending plus general and life insurance through one listed vehicle, with a smaller set of emerging businesses (health, digital, asset management) still in investment/loss-making mode.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (Q4 YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Q4 consolidated total income growth was held down by mark-to-market losses on debt and equity portfolios (linked to Middle East conflict-driven market volatility). Excluding those MTM losses, the company says revenue growth would have been 14% — the underlying operating trend is stronger than the headline.

pat_growth_pct.label: PAT Growth (Q4 YoY)
pat_growth_pct.note: Q4 consolidated PAT ₹2,539 crore. Full-year FY26 PAT grew a similar 10% to ₹9,801 crore — solid but far below the group's historical 20%+ growth pace, reflecting a genuinely tougher year for the underlying financial-services businesses.

aum_growth_pct.label: Bajaj Finance AUM Growth
aum_growth_pct.note: The lending arm (Bajaj Finance) remains the key growth engine — AUM grew 22% YoY to ₹5.09 lakh crore, with Q4 net total income up 21%. Lending is carrying the group while insurance disappoints.

roe_pct.label: Return on Equity
roe_pct.note: A modest figure for a financial-services group, reflecting the holding-company structure (value is spread across subsidiaries) plus drag from loss-making emerging businesses (₹539 crore of losses in FY26).

bull: The lending arm (Bajaj Finance) delivered strongly — AUM up 22% YoY to ₹5.09 lakh crore with Q4 net total income up 21% — genuinely carrying group growth while other segments disappointed, a proven, market-leading consumer-lending franchise.

Excluding one-off mark-to-market portfolio losses from market volatility, Q4 revenue growth would have been 14% (versus the 6% reported) — the underlying operating momentum is materially stronger than the headline number suggests.

Full ownership of the insurance businesses (Bajaj bought out Allianz's 24-year JV stake for ~₹21,390 crore) gives the group complete strategic control and flexibility over product launches and decisions for the first time — a genuine structural change, not just a shareholding reshuffle.
bear: The general insurance arm genuinely disappointed — Q4 Gross Written Premium was flat (~9% below analyst targets) and Q4 profit grew just 2% (26% below targets) — a real, current weak spot in one of the group's three core businesses, at a time the insurance industry is expected to grow ~10% CAGR.

Full-year PAT growth of 10% is well below the group's historical 20%+ pace, and independent analysts flag margin pressure across lending and insurance from elevated funding costs and regulatory scrutiny on unsecured lending — a genuinely tougher operating environment, not a one-quarter blip.

Now that Bajaj fully owns the insurance businesses, it carries 100% of their execution risk directly — the same insurance weakness that showed up this quarter now flows entirely to Bajaj Finserv rather than being shared with a JV partner, and emerging businesses (health, digital, AMC) remain loss-making (₹539 crore drag in FY26).

value_chain.position: Sits at the top of a diversified financial-services group as a holding company — it doesn't directly originate loans or underwrite policies itself, but captures the aggregate value of India's leading consumer-lending NBFC (Bajaj Finance) plus now-wholly-owned general and life insurers, giving investors bundled exposure to both credit-risk-bearing lending and insurance underwriting in one vehicle.
value_chain.note: The buyout of Allianz's stake marks a strategic pivot from a joint-venture model (shared control, shared risk) to full ownership (full control, full risk) — greater strategic flexibility, but also greater concentration of both upside and execution risk in Bajaj Finserv's own hands.

tech_geo_tags.label: Insurance underperformance is a real, current weak spot — flat general-insurance GWP and missed Q4 profit targets, now fully borne by Bajaj Finserv following the Allianz buyout rather than shared with a JV partner
Regulatory scrutiny on unsecured lending plus elevated funding costs are squeezing margins across the lending business industry-wide — a structural, sector-level pressure, not company-specific

India's insurance market is projected for significant multi-decade expansion, and full ownership now gives Bajaj Finserv complete strategic flexibility to capture that growth with faster product launches and decisions

BAJAJFINSV.moat_note: A rare, genuinely diversified financial-services platform under one roof — a market-leading consumer-lending NBFC (Bajaj Finance) plus now-wholly-owned general and life insurers — combined with a strong brand and one of India's widest financial-product distribution reaches. The moat is real, but the holding-company structure means investors get diluted, indirect exposure to each business, and this year's insurance weakness shows the diversification cuts both ways: no single strong business fully offsets a weak one at the group level.

JIOFIN.name: Jio Financial Services
JIOFIN.as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
JIOFIN.source_note: Jio Financial's own Q4 FY26 results and earnings call, cross-checked against Univest, Medianama, AlphaSpread and Whalesbook coverage
JIOFIN.business_core: Jio Financial Services (JFS), demerged from Reliance Industries in 2023, is a Mukesh Ambani-led financial-services platform building four verticals in parallel — lending (Jio Credit/Jio Finance), payments (Jio Payments Bank, Jio Payment Solutions), asset management (the Jio BlackRock JV) and insurance broking/underwriting. Its entire thesis rests on cross-selling financial products to Reliance's 450+ million Jio telecom subscriber base via a digital-first app. It's a genuine early-stage buildout: revenue is scaling explosively but profit is deliberately depressed by heavy investment.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (Q4 YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Q4 revenue from operations nearly doubled to ₹1,018.51 crore. FY26 total income (ex-dividends) rose 78% to ₹3,274 crore — genuinely explosive top-line scaling off a small base, driven by lending, payments and insurance broking all ramping at once.

pat_growth_pct.label: PAT Growth (Q4 YoY)
pat_growth_pct.note: Consolidated Q4 PAT fell to ₹272.22 crore. The decline is a deliberate growth-vs-profitability trade-off: total expenses rose 327% YoY on heavy business-building investment, plus Jio Payments Bank's operating loss now consolidates fully into JFS's books (it became a 100% subsidiary in June 2025).

aum_growth_pct.label: Lending AUM Growth (Jio Credit)
aum_growth_pct.note: Jio Credit AUM surged to ₹25,711 crore, up 156% YoY (a ~149x jump since FY24 off a near-zero base), driven entirely by organic disbursements. Home loans/LAP are 45% of the book, corporate loans 44%.

roe_pct.label: Return on Equity
roe_pct.note: Not meaningful yet — JFS carries a very large ₹1.33 lakh crore net worth (much of it from the Reliance demerger) against still-small early-stage profits, so a computed RoE would understate the business and mislead. Shown as — rather than a distorted figure.

bull: Revenue nearly doubled YoY (Q4 +106.5%, FY26 +78%) with lending AUM up 156% — genuinely explosive scaling across lending, payments, asset management and insurance broking simultaneously, all driven by organic growth rather than acquisitions.

Core operating income now drives 54% of net total income (up from 20% a year ago) — a real, structural shift away from passive treasury/dividend income toward a genuine operating financial-services business, exactly the maturation the thesis requires.

The Jio BlackRock AMC reached ₹15,200+ crore AUM with over 40% of inflows from beyond India's top 30 cities — evidence the Reliance-scale distribution advantage (450M+ Jio subscribers, 19,000+ pin codes) is translating into genuine financial-product reach, not just a theoretical edge.
bear: Consolidated PAT fell 14% YoY as total expenses surged 327% — this is a company deliberately sacrificing near-term profit for growth, which means investors are underwriting 3-5 years of continued execution before profitability catches up to the revenue scaling, with no guarantee it does.

At a ~₹1.55 lakh crore market cap against current profitability, the valuation prices in substantial future success that hasn't yet been proven — the buildout is real, but so is the execution risk in a fiercely competitive digital-lending and payments market.

Treasury volatility (from late-March market/geopolitical stress) hit both treasury income and JioBlackRock's AUM this quarter — a reminder that a meaningful part of the current asset base is market-linked and exposed to downturns while the core operating businesses are still too young to fully offset it.

value_chain.position: Building an integrated, digital-first financial-services stack (Borrow / Invest / Transact / Protect) that sits directly on top of Reliance's existing consumer ecosystem — the differentiator isn't any single product but the ability to cross-sell lending, payments, mutual funds and insurance to a captive 450M+ Jio subscriber base through one AI-driven app, a distribution advantage no standalone fintech can match.
value_chain.note: Strategic partnerships anchor each vertical: BlackRock for asset management, Allianz for reinsurance and upcoming general/life insurance — JFS is combining Reliance's distribution reach with established global partners' product expertise rather than building every capability from scratch.

tech_geo_tags.label: Deep growth-stage unprofitability: PAT falling 14% while expenses surge 327% means investors are underwriting years of continued heavy investment before profitability matches the revenue scaling — real execution risk over a multi-year horizon
Intense competition in India's digital-lending and payments markets means the 49% disbursement growth and rapid AUM ramp must be sustained against well-funded fintech and bank rivals — not guaranteed

The Reliance ecosystem cross-sell opportunity (450M+ Jio subscribers, 19,000+ pin codes, 23M app users) plus marquee partnerships (BlackRock, Allianz) give JFS a genuinely differentiated distribution-and-expertise combination few fintechs can replicate

JIOFIN.moat_note: The core moat is potential rather than proven: privileged access to Reliance's 450M+ Jio subscriber base is a genuinely unique distribution advantage no standalone fintech can replicate, and marquee partnerships (BlackRock for AMC, Allianz for insurance) add credible product expertise. But unlike an incumbent like Bajaj Finance with a decades-proven lending book, JFS's moat is still a thesis being tested — the explosive revenue growth is real evidence it's working, but durable, profitable competitive advantage is still years of execution away from being confirmed.

ETERNAL.name: Eternal (formerly Zomato)
ETERNAL.as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
ETERNAL.source_note: Eternal's own Q4 FY26 results and shareholder letter, cross-checked against Sahi.com, Business Standard, Angel One and Quash coverage
ETERNAL.business_core: Eternal (renamed from Zomato) is India's leading consumer-internet platform spanning four businesses: Zomato (food delivery), Blinkit (quick commerce — 10-minute grocery/essentials delivery, now the company's biggest growth engine), Hyperpure (B2B restaurant supplies) and District (going-out/events). Because Blinkit shifted from a marketplace model to an inventory-led (1P) model in FY26, reported revenue is mechanically inflated — Net Order Value (NOV, the total value of goods/orders flowing through the platform) is the cleaner metric to judge real business scale, not headline revenue.

nov_growth_pct.label: B2C Net Order Value Growth (Q4 YoY)
nov_growth_pct.note: B2C NOV reached ₹26,880 crore. This is the cleanest measure of real platform scale — Blinkit's NOV alone surged 95.4% YoY to ₹14,386 crore, now exceeding Zomato food delivery's NOV. Food delivery NOV grew a steadier 18.8%.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Adjusted Revenue Growth (Q4 YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Like-for-like adjusted revenue growth of 64% is the honest figure. The headline reported revenue growth of 196% (to ₹17,292 crore) is mechanically inflated by Blinkit's marketplace-to-inventory (1P) accounting shift — reading the headline number badly overstates real growth.

adj_ebitda_growth_pct.label: Adjusted EBITDA Growth (Q4 YoY)
adj_ebitda_growth_pct.note: Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA ₹429 crore. The single most important milestone this quarter: Blinkit turned Adjusted-EBITDA positive for the first time (₹37 crore) — the quick-commerce business is now demonstrably profitable at scale, not just growing.

pat_growth_pct.label: PAT Growth (Q4 YoY)
pat_growth_pct.note: Q4 PAT ₹174 crore, well above estimates. But full-year FY26 PAT actually fell to ₹366 crore (from ₹527 crore) — a heavier investment phase earlier in the year (especially Q1) means the strong Q4 doesn't represent the full-year trend. The quarterly trajectory (Q1 loss → ₹102cr Q3 → ₹174cr Q4) is the more relevant signal.

bull: Blinkit turned Adjusted-EBITDA positive for the first time (₹37 crore) while NOV surged 95.4% YoY — the single biggest milestone this quarter, proving quick commerce can translate explosive scale into actual operating profit, not just growth.

109 million Indians transacted over $10 billion through the platform in FY26, with Blinkit's NOV compounding at 104% CAGR from FY23-26 — genuine, category-defining scale, and management targets $1 billion in Adjusted EBITDA by FY29 (roughly 8x in five years from an already-profitable base).

Quick commerce is still concentrated in the top 15-20 cities and a narrow product range (Delhi NCR offers ~80,000 SKUs versus ~20,000 in newer markets) — geographic expansion, assortment deepening and demand densification are all still early innings, each compounding on the others.
bear: Full-year FY26 PAT actually declined to ₹366 crore (from ₹527 crore) despite the explosive revenue growth — profitability is being deliberately sacrificed for expansion, so investors are underwriting continued execution rather than banking current earnings.

The headline 196% reported revenue growth is mechanically inflated by Blinkit's marketplace-to-inventory accounting shift — the honest like-for-like figure is 64%, and reading the headline number badly overstates the real underlying growth rate.

Competitive intensity in quick commerce is genuinely fierce and repeatedly flagged by analysts — Zepto and other well-funded rivals are building speed and density, meaning Blinkit's lead and margins must be defended continuously against deep-pocketed competition, not assumed as durable.

value_chain.position: Operates asset-heavier than a pure marketplace now — Blinkit's shift to an inventory-led (1P) model and its 2,243 dark stores mean Eternal increasingly owns inventory and last-mile fulfilment infrastructure directly, capturing more margin per order but also carrying more operational and working-capital risk than a commission-only marketplace.
value_chain.note: The four businesses (Zomato food delivery, Blinkit quick commerce, Hyperpure B2B supplies, District going-out) each sit at different points of maturity — Blinkit is the growth engine, food delivery the steady profitable base, and District/Hyperpure smaller optionality plays still being built.

tech_geo_tags.label: Fierce quick-commerce competition (Zepto and other well-funded rivals) means Blinkit's lead and newly-achieved profitability must be continuously defended — a genuine, ongoing competitive risk, not a settled position

Deliberate profitability sacrifice for growth: full-year PAT fell despite explosive revenue, so investors are underwriting a multi-year path to the FY29 $1bn Adjusted-EBITDA target rather than current earnings

Quick commerce is still early-innings geographically and by assortment (concentrated in top cities, narrow SKU range outside metros) — a long structural runway of expansion, densification and category deepening ahead, each compounding the others

ETERNAL.moat_note: Genuine network effects and scale in both food delivery (a two-sided marketplace of restaurants and diners built over 18 years) and quick commerce (Blinkit's dark-store density and supply-chain efficiency, now demonstrably profitable). The moat is real and Blinkit's first-ever positive EBITDA is strong evidence it's durable at scale — but quick commerce is a young, capital-intensive, fiercely contested category, so the moat's durability is still being actively tested against well-funded rivals rather than fully established.

TRENT.name: Trent
TRENT.as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
TRENT.source_note: Trent's own Q4 & FY26 results release, cross-checked against ICICI Direct Research, HDFC Sky and MarketsMojo coverage
TRENT.business_core: Trent (Tata Group) is one of India's fastest-growing fashion and value retailers, operating premium family-fashion stores (Westside), a rapidly expanding value-fashion format (Zudio, its main growth engine, approaching 1,000 stores) and a grocery/essentials business (Star). Growth is driven overwhelmingly by aggressive new-store rollout — especially Zudio into Tier 2/3 cities — making store additions and like-for-like (same-store) sales the two metrics that together reveal whether growth is genuine expansion or just running to stand still.

revenue_growth_pct.label: Revenue Growth (Q4 YoY)
revenue_growth_pct.note: Consolidated revenue ₹5,028 crore. Driven almost entirely by store-count expansion (retail area +30% YoY) rather than existing-store productivity — revenue per square foot actually declined ~6% as like-for-like growth stayed in low single digits.

ebitda_margin_pct.label: EBITDA Margin (Q4)
ebitda_margin_pct.note: Expanded 242bps YoY, driven by gross-margin improvement (better mix, lower discounting) plus lower employee and rent cost per square foot. Note it fell sequentially from Q3's ~20.2% on seasonal softness.

store_growth_pct.label: Retail Area Growth (YoY)
store_growth_pct.note: Total retail footprint grew 30% YoY to ~17.7 million sq ft. Trent added 60 Westside and 212 Zudio stores in FY26 (~1.2 Zudio stores per day in Q4), with ~80% of new Zudio stores in Tier 2/3 cities.

pat_growth_pct.label: Adjusted PAT Growth (Q4 YoY)
pat_growth_pct.note: Adjusted PAT ₹455 crore (standalone). Full-year FY26 PAT grew a more modest 13-25% depending on the measure and labour-code adjustment. Trent also announced its first-ever bonus issue (1:2) — a management confidence signal.

bull: Explosive, disciplined store expansion — 212 new Zudio stores in FY26 (~1.2 per day in Q4), with ~80% of new stores in Tier 2/3 cities targeting underpenetrated, faster-growing markets — a genuine, repeatable growth engine, not a one-off.

EBITDA margin expanded 242bps YoY in Q4 even while expanding aggressively, driven by better product mix, lower discounting, and falling employee/rent cost per square foot (aided by RFID supply-chain integration) — real operating leverage kicking in as scale builds.

Emerging categories (beauty, innerwear, footwear) now contribute over 21% of revenue and Westside online grew 25% — genuine diversification beyond core apparel, plus a maiden 1:2 bonus issue signalling management's confidence in the medium-term outlook.
bear: Like-for-like (same-store) growth stayed in low single digits and revenue per square foot actually declined ~6% YoY — the impressive 19% headline revenue growth is almost entirely new-store-driven, meaning the existing store base is barely growing, a genuine quality-of-growth concern.

The stock trades at a very rich ~95x P/E (per one analysis) with a PEG around 8x — a valuation leaving minimal room for execution missteps, especially as Q4 PAT fell ~22% sequentially and FII holdings declined from ~19.7% to ~15.6% over the year.

Management explicitly flagged cautious discretionary consumer sentiment (geopolitical uncertainty, inflation, cost-of-living concerns) softening as Q4 progressed — a real demand-side headwind for a value-fashion retailer whose growth depends on continued consumer willingness to spend.

value_chain.position: A vertically-engaged fashion retailer — Trent designs and sources most of its own private-label merchandise (Westside and Zudio are almost entirely own-brand, unlike department stores reselling third-party labels), controls pricing and inventory tightly, and sells through its own rapidly expanding owned/leased store network, capturing more margin than a multi-brand reseller.
value_chain.note: The heavy own-brand/private-label model (own-brand share in Star grocery rose to 73%) is central to the margin story — private labels generate higher margins, create exclusivity, and drive repeat purchases since the products are only available in Trent's own stores.

tech_geo_tags.label: Low single-digit like-for-like growth and declining revenue per square foot mean growth is almost entirely dependent on continued aggressive store rollout — if new-store economics or the expansion pace falter, the growth story is more exposed than the headline revenue number suggests

Cautious discretionary consumer sentiment (flagged by management, tied to geopolitical/inflation uncertainty) is a real, current demand-side headwind for a value-fashion retailer, plus a rich valuation that leaves little room for disappointment

Deep, underpenetrated Tier 2/3 city expansion runway (~80% of new Zudio stores) combined with genuine operating leverage as scale builds gives a long structural growth path in India's organised-value-retail shift

TRENT.moat_note: A powerful own-brand/private-label model (Zudio and Westside are almost entirely proprietary merchandise) combined with fast, disciplined store-rollout execution gives Trent a genuine cost and exclusivity moat in India's organised value-retail shift — Zudio in particular has proven a durable, hard-to-replicate value-fashion format. The moat is real, but the low like-for-like growth and premium valuation mean the market is pricing in near-flawless continued execution of the expansion story rather than just the strength of the existing base.
